title: Zechariah
author: Zechariah son of Berechiah, a priest who prophesied alongside Haggai
setting: Jerusalem immediately after Haggai's ministry, with the temple under construction. The book contains eight night visions (chs. 1–6) and then two burdens (chs. 9–14) with extensive messianic prophecy.
purpose: To encourage the post-exilic community with visions of God's sovereign purposes for Jerusalem, to announce the coming of the messianic king (9:9 — quoted at the Triumphal Entry), and to paint an apocalyptic picture of the final victory of God.
themes: God's jealousy for Jerusalem and his people
The high priest Joshua as a type of the Messiah
The triumphal king on a donkey (9:9)
The cosmic battle and the LORD's coming as king

outline.label: Questions about Fasting; Promises of Restoration
Second Burden: The Day of the LORD

before.label: God's glory departs from Jerusalem — Ezekiel's vision
First exiles return; temple foundation laid
Haggai — temple rebuilding resumes under God's word

after.label: Second temple completed and dedicated to the LORD
Jesus rides into Jerusalem on a donkey — Zech 9:9 fulfilled
Revelation — the pierced one (Zech 12:10) returns in glory

key_people.name: Zechariah ben Berechiah
key_people.role: Post-exilic prophet contemporary with Haggai (520–518 BC for the dated oracles; possibly later for chs. 9–14); his visions are the most complex in the OT after Ezekiel

key_people.name: Joshua the High Priest
key_people.role: Central figure in visions 3 and 4 (chs. 3–4); his cleansing and coronation as high priest prefigures the coming Branch who will unite priestly and royal functions

key_people.name: Zerubbabel
key_people.role: Governor of Judah whose temple-completion is the occasion for the vision of the lampstand (ch. 4) and the oracle 'Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit'

key_people.name: The Branch
key_people.role: The messianic title (3:8; 6:12) applied to a coming figure who will build the true temple and bear royal honour — one of Zechariah's most developed messianic portraits

context: Zechariah began his ministry in 520 BC, the same year as Haggai, and the dated oracles cover 520–518 BC. Chapters 9–14, which are undated and stylistically distinct, are often called Deutero-Zechariah and may come from a later period. Together the book forms the longest of the minor prophets. Zechariah's visions address the discouraged post-exilic community with cosmic assurances: God has returned to Zion, will punish the nations that oppressed Israel, and will establish his kingdom.
christ_connection: Zechariah is quoted more in the NT passion narratives than any other OT book. Specific fulfilments include: Jesus riding into Jerusalem on a donkey (9:9; Matt 21:5; John 12:15), thirty pieces of silver thrown into the house of the LORD (11:12–13; Matt 26:15; 27:9–10), striking the shepherd and scattering the sheep (13:7; Matt 26:31; Mark 14:27), and the pierced one for whom Jerusalem mourns (12:10; John 19:37; Rev 1:7). The Branch who builds the temple and unites priestly and royal offices (6:12–13) is fulfilled in Christ as both king and eternal high priest.

key_verses.note: 'Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says the LORD of hosts' — the theological principle that God's purposes are accomplished by his Spirit through human weakness, not military or political power
'Behold, your king is coming to you; righteous and having salvation is he, humble and mounted on a donkey' — fulfilled at the Triumphal Entry (Matt 21:5; John 12:15)
'They will look on me, on him whom they have pierced, and they shall mourn for him' — applied to Jesus' crucifixion in John 19:37 and to his return in Revelation 1:7

themes_detail.title: The Return of God's Presence to Zion
themes_detail.text: The eight night visions (chs. 1–6) all revolve around the theme of God's return to Jerusalem: he has been angry but is now compassionate; the nations that oppressed Israel will be judged; the temple will be rebuilt; the high priest will be cleansed; God will dwell in Jerusalem's midst. This is the direct reversal of Ezekiel's vision of God's glory departing from the temple (Ezek 10–11). The eschatological trajectory is clear: history moves toward God's full and permanent return to his people — fulfilled in the Incarnation and consummated in the New Jerusalem.

themes_detail.title: Messianic Hope: The Branch and the Shepherd-King
themes_detail.text: Zechariah develops several distinct messianic figures that converge on a single coming one: the Branch who will build the temple (3:8; 6:12–13), the humble king on a donkey (9:9), the shepherd struck by God's sword (13:7), the thirty-piece-of-silver figure (11:12–13), and the pierced one (12:10). That these all find fulfilment in Jesus is not a later imposition but the internal logic of the book: they are aspects of one complex figure who will unite what the present era divides — priest and king, humble and powerful, rejected and vindicated.

themes_detail.title: Not by Might but by the Spirit
themes_detail.text: Zechariah 4:6 — delivered to Zerubbabel about the temple completion — establishes the principle by which God works: not through military or political power but through his Spirit working through weak and discouraged human instruments. The lampstand fed by olive trees (4:2–6) is the image: the light burns continuously because it is supplied by a source beyond human effort. This is the NT principle of ministry: 'power is made perfect in weakness' (2 Cor 12:9), and the gospel advances not through the impressive but through the cruciform.

themes_detail.title: Apocalyptic Hope and the Final Battle
themes_detail.text: Chapters 12–14 describe a climactic eschatological battle in which Jerusalem is surrounded by all nations, the inhabitants mourn over the pierced one, and God himself fights for Jerusalem. The imagery is cosmic and not straightforwardly historical, belonging to the tradition of apocalyptic literature that Revelation inherits. The key theological claims are: God will complete what he has promised despite all opposition; the nations' assault on Jerusalem is the assault on God himself; and the final state is one of holy worship — 'the LORD will be king over all the earth; on that day the LORD will be one and his name one' (14:9).
